summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

The logistic equation is dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K). If r = 0.4 and K = 500, what is dP/dt when P = 250?

dP/dt = 0.4(250)(1 - 250/500) = 0.4(250)(0.5) = 50.

In the logistic model dP/dt = 0.3P(1 - P/1000), what is the carrying capacity?

In dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K), the carrying capacity is K. Here K = 1000, which is the maximum sustainable population.

For the logistic equation dP/dt = 2P(1 - P/100), what is the growth rate dP/dt when P = 75?

dP/dt = 2(75)(1 - 75/100) = 150(0.25) = 37.5.

What is 'logistic growth'?

Logistic growth modifies exponential growth by including a limiting factor. The growth rate slows as the population P approaches the carrying capacity K, described by dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K).

What is the 'carrying capacity' in a logistic model?

The carrying capacity K represents the maximum population the environment can support. As P approaches K, resources become limited and growth slows to zero.

What is the 'inflection point' of a logistic curve?

At P = K/2, the logistic curve has an inflection point: the curve changes from concave up (accelerating growth) to concave down (decelerating growth). The growth rate dP/dt reaches its maximum here.

What is the difference between 'exponential growth' and 'logistic growth'?

Exponential growth dP/dt = rP has no limiting factor and grows without bound. Logistic growth dP/dt = rP(1-P/K) includes density dependence through the factor (1-P/K), which limits the population to the carrying capacity K.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: An ecologist manages a forest where tree count follows logistic growth with K = 10,000 and r = 0.2. Currently there are 8,000 trees. The ecologist plans to harvest 200 trees per year. Will this harvest rate be sustainable?

Current growth: dP/dt = 0.2(8000)(1 - 8000/10000) = 0.2(8000)(0.2) = 320 trees/year. Maximum sustainable yield is at P = 5000: dP/dt_max = 0.2(5000)(0.5) = 500. Since 200 < 500, harvesting 200/year is sustainable and the population will adjust to a new equilibrium.
